127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). \chapter{Abelian-variety rigidity: morphisms from a genus-$0$ curve into an abelian variety are constant}
\label{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}
% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects.lean AlgebraicJacobian/RigidityLemma.lean

% Local macro: diagrammatic (Lean `≫`) composition. Defined locally because this
% chapter is the writer's only editable file; flagged in the writer report for promotion
% to macros/common.tex if desired. \providecommand is a no-op if it is later added globally.
\providecommand{\fatsemi}{\mathbin{\,;\,}}

% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-157). This chapter is the dedicated home of the project's COMMITTED
% genus-0 route (route (c)): the characteristic-free abelian-variety rigidity stack. It is
% 1:1 with the new upstream Lean file AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean, which
% imports AlgebraicJacobian.Genus and is imported by AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian (breaking the
% RigidityKbar -> Rigidity -> Jacobian import cycle so that genusZeroWitness can consume the
% keystone directly). The headline thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV is the char-free
% replacement (no [CharZero]) for the over-k-bar declaration rigidity_over_kbar of
% \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (which remains in tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and
% still carries [CharZero]). The df=0 / H^0(P^1,Omega)=0 / Serre-duality framing is the
% fallback (a), NOT the route developed here.

The genus-$0$ arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
($J = \Spec k$); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$
into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field $k$, so the
cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
O(-2)) = 0$, hence $df = 0$, hence $f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
characteristic-$p$ Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant $f$ can have $df = 0$. The
rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.

The chain has two parts. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, Mumford,
Form~I; equivalently Milne's Rigidity Theorem~1.1) is an elementary properness/closed-map
argument --- it uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, is the entry point
for formalisation, and is now proven axiom-clean. From the Rigidity Lemma, Milne's \S I.3
derives the genus-$0$ base case \emph{without} the theorem of the cube --- and, in this
project's reading, \emph{without} Milne's Theorem~3.2 (rational-map extension), Lemma~3.3,
Auslander--Buchsbaum, or differentials (the ``4th route''; see
\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). The decisive observation is that $\mathbb P^1$ is
\emph{already complete} and $f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ is \emph{already a total morphism}, so
there is no rational defect map to extend over a complete surface and Theorem~3.2 never enters.
Instead the base case is closed by the \textbf{$\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut} (the project's
primary route, an Archon-original realisation of Milne Proposition~3.9): one uses the
\emph{multiplicative scaling} action $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to
\mathbb P^1$, $(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x$ (the M\"obius scaling fixing both $0$ and
$\infty$), which is a \emph{genuine total} scheme morphism on all of $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb
G_m$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}). Normalising $f(0) = \eta_A$ and composing $h := \sigma_\times
\fatsemi f$, one applies additivity of $\mathrm{Hom}(-, A)$ over a product
(\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires
only the \emph{first} factor complete --- with $V = \mathbb P^1$ proper, $W = \mathbb G_m$, and
base points $0 \in \mathbb P^1$, $1 \in \mathbb G_m$. Because $0$ is a \emph{fixed point} of

309	\section{Relative spectrum via glueing}
310	\label{section-spec-via-glueing}
311	
312	\begin{situation}
313	\label{situation-relative-spec}
314	Here $S$ is a scheme, and $\mathcal{A}$ is a quasi-coherent
315	$\mathcal{O}_S$-algebra. This means that $\mathcal{A}$ is a
316	sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
317	$\mathcal{O}_S$-module.
318	\end{situation}
319	
320	\noindent
321	In this section we outline how to construct a morphism
322	of schemes
323	$$
324	\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
325	$$
326	by glueing the spectra $\Spec(\Gamma(U, \mathcal{A}))$
327	where $U$ ranges over the affine opens of $S$. We first show that the
328	spectra of the values of $\mathcal{A}$ over affines form a
329	suitable collection of schemes, as in Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-glueing}.
330	
331	\begin{lemma}
332	\label{lemma-spec-inclusion}
333	In Situation \ref{situation-relative-spec}.
334	Suppose $U \subset U' \subset S$ are affine opens.
335	Let $A = \mathcal{A}(U)$ and $A' = \mathcal{A}(U')$.
336	The map of rings $A' \to A$ induces a morphism
337	$\Spec(A) \to \Spec(A')$, and the diagram
338	$$
339	\xymatrix{
340	\Spec(A) \ar[r] \ar[d] &
341	\Spec(A') \ar[d] \\
342	U \ar[r] &
343	U'
344	}
345	$$
346	is cartesian.
347	\end{lemma}
348	
349	\begin{proof}
350	Let $R = \mathcal{O}_S(U)$ and $R' = \mathcal{O}_S(U')$.
351	Note that the map $R \otimes_{R'} A' \to A$ is an isomorphism as
352	$\mathcal{A}$ is quasi-coherent
353	(see Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback} for example).
354	The result follows from the description of the fibre product of
355	affine schemes in
356	Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-fibre-product-affine-schemes}.
357	\end{proof}
358	
359	\noindent
360	In particular the morphism $\Spec(A) \to \Spec(A')$
361	of the lemma is an open immersion.
362	
363	\begin{lemma}
364	\label{lemma-transitive-spec}
365	In Situation \ref{situation-relative-spec}.
366	Suppose $U \subset U' \subset U'' \subset S$ are affine opens.
367	Let $A = \mathcal{A}(U)$, $A' = \mathcal{A}(U')$ and $A'' = \mathcal{A}(U'')$.
368	The composition of the morphisms
369	$\Spec(A) \to \Spec(A')$, and
370	$\Spec(A') \to \Spec(A'')$ of
371	Lemma \ref{lemma-spec-inclusion} gives the
372	morphism $\Spec(A) \to \Spec(A'')$
373	of Lemma \ref{lemma-spec-inclusion}.
374	\end{lemma}
375	
376	\begin{proof}
377	This follows as the map $A'' \to A$ is the composition of $A'' \to A'$ and
378	$A' \to A$ (because $\mathcal{A}$ is a sheaf).
379	\end{proof}
380	
381	\begin{lemma}
382	\label{lemma-glue-relative-spec}
383	In Situation \ref{situation-relative-spec}.
384	There exists a morphism of schemes
385	$$
386	\pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
387	$$
388	with the following properties:
389	\begin{enumerate}
390	\item for every affine open $U \subset S$ there exists an isomorphism
391	$i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))$ over $U$, and
392	\item for $U \subset U' \subset S$ affine open the composition
393	$$
394	\xymatrix{
395	\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
396	\pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
397	\pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
398	\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
399	}
400	$$
401	is the open immersion of Lemma \ref{lemma-spec-inclusion} above.
402	\end{enumerate}
403	Moreover, $\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})$
404	is unique up to unique isomorphism over $S$.
405	\end{lemma}

408	Follows immediately from
409	Lemmas \ref{lemma-relative-glueing},
410	\ref{lemma-spec-inclusion}, and
411	\ref{lemma-transitive-spec}.
412	Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
413	Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-glueing}.
414	\end{proof}

427	\section{Relative spectrum as a functor}
428	\label{section-spec}
429	
430	\noindent
431	We place ourselves in Situation \ref{situation-relative-spec}, i.e.,
432	$S$ is a scheme and $\mathcal{A}$ is a quasi-coherent sheaf of
433	$\mathcal{O}_S$-algebras.
434	
435	\medskip\noindent
436	For any $f : T \to S$ the pullback
437	$f^*\mathcal{A}$ is a quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_T$-algebras.
438	We are going to consider pairs $(f : T \to S, \varphi)$ where
439	$f$ is a morphism of schemes and $\varphi : f^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T$
440	is a morphism of $\mathcal{O}_T$-algebras. Note that this is the
441	same as giving a $f^{-1}\mathcal{O}_S$-algebra homomorphism
442	$\varphi : f^{-1}\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T$, see
443	Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-adjointness-tensor-restrict}.
444	This is also the same as giving an $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebra map
445	$\varphi : \mathcal{A} \to f_*\mathcal{O}_T$, see
446	Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-adjoint-push-pull-modules}.
447	We will use all three ways of thinking about $\varphi$,
448	without further mention.
449	
450	\medskip\noindent
451	Given such a
452	pair $(f : T \to S, \varphi)$ and a morphism $a : T' \to T$ we get
453	a second pair $(f' = f \circ a, \varphi' = a^*\varphi)$ which we
454	call the pullback of $(f, \varphi)$. One way to describe
455	$\varphi' = a^*\varphi$ is as the composition
456	$\mathcal{A} \to f_*\mathcal{O}_T \to f'_*\mathcal{O}_{T'}$
457	where the second map is $f_*a^\sharp$ with
458	$a^\sharp : \mathcal{O}_T \to a_*\mathcal{O}_{T'}$.
459	In this way we have defined a functor
460	\begin{eqnarray}
461	\label{equation-spec}
462	F : \Sch^{opp} & \longrightarrow & \textit{Sets} \\
463	T & \longmapsto & F(T) = \{\text{pairs }(f, \varphi) \text{ as above}\}
464	\nonumber
465	\end{eqnarray}
466	
467	\begin{lemma}
468	\label{lemma-spec-base-change}
469	In Situation \ref{situation-relative-spec}.
470	Let $F$ be the functor
471	associated to $(S, \mathcal{A})$ above.
472	Let $g : S' \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
473	Set $\mathcal{A}' = g^*\mathcal{A}$. Let $F'$ be the
474	functor associated to $(S', \mathcal{A}')$ above.
475	Then there is a canonical isomorphism
476	$$
477	F' \cong h_{S'} \times_{h_S} F
478	$$
479	of functors.
480	\end{lemma}
481	
482	\begin{proof}
483	A pair $(f' : T \to S', \varphi' : (f')^*\mathcal{A}' \to \mathcal{O}_T)$
484	is the same as a pair $(f, \varphi : f^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T)$
485	together with a factorization of $f$ as $f = g \circ f'$. Namely with
486	this notation we have
487	$(f')^* \mathcal{A}' = (f')^*g^*\mathcal{A} = f^*\mathcal{A}$.
488	Hence the lemma.
489	\end{proof}
490	
491	\begin{lemma}
492	\label{lemma-spec-affine}
493	In Situation \ref{situation-relative-spec}.
494	Let $F$ be the functor associated to $(S, \mathcal{A})$ above.
495	If $S$ is affine, then $F$ is representable by the
496	affine scheme $\Spec(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{A}))$.
497	\end{lemma}
498	
499	\begin{proof}
500	Write $S = \Spec(R)$ and $A = \Gamma(S, \mathcal{A})$.
501	Then $A$ is an $R$-algebra and $\mathcal{A} = \widetilde A$.
502	The ring map $R \to A$ gives rise to a canonical map
503	$$
504	f_{univ} : \Spec(A)
505	\longrightarrow
506	S = \Spec(R).
507	$$
508	We have
509	$f_{univ}^*\mathcal{A} =  \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}$
510	by Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback}.
511	Hence there is a canonical map
512	$$
513	\varphi_{univ} :
514	f_{univ}^*\mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}
515	\longrightarrow
516	\widetilde A = \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(A)}
517	$$
518	coming from the $A$-module map $A \otimes_R A \to A$,
519	$a \otimes a' \mapsto aa'$. We claim that the pair
520	$(f_{univ}, \varphi_{univ})$ represents $F$ in this case.
521	In other words we claim that for any scheme $T$ the map
522	$$
523	\Mor(T, \Spec(A)) \longrightarrow \{\text{pairs } (f, \varphi)\},\quad
524	a \longmapsto (f_{univ} \circ a, a^*\varphi_{univ})
525	$$
526	is bijective.
527	
528	\medskip\noindent
529	Let us construct the inverse map.
530	For any pair $(f : T \to S, \varphi)$ we get the induced
531	ring map
532	$$
533	\xymatrix{
534	A = \Gamma(S, \mathcal{A}) \ar[r]^{f^*} &
535	\Gamma(T, f^*\mathcal{A}) \ar[r]^{\varphi} &
536	\Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)
537	}
538	$$
539	This induces a morphism of schemes $T \to \Spec(A)$
540	by Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-morphism-into-affine}.
541	
542	\medskip\noindent
543	The verification that this map is inverse to the map
544	displayed above is omitted.
545	\end{proof}
546	
547	\begin{lemma}
548	\label{lemma-spec}
549	In Situation \ref{situation-relative-spec}.
550	The functor $F$ is representable by a scheme.
551	\end{lemma}
552	
553	\begin{proof}
554	We are going to use Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-glue-functors}.
555	
556	\medskip\noindent
557	First we check that $F$ satisfies the sheaf property for the
558	Zariski topology. Namely, suppose that $T$ is a scheme,
559	that $T = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ is an open covering,
560	and that $(f_i, \varphi_i) \in F(U_i)$ such that
561	$(f_i, \varphi_i)|_{U_i \cap U_j} = (f_j, \varphi_j)|_{U_i \cap U_j}$.
562	This implies that the morphisms $f_i : U_i \to S$
563	glue to a morphism of schemes $f : T \to S$ such that
564	$f|_{U_i} = f_i$, see Schemes, Section \ref{schemes-section-glueing-schemes}.
565	Thus $f_i^*\mathcal{A} = f^*\mathcal{A}|_{U_i}$ and by assumption the
566	morphisms $\varphi_i$ agree on $U_i \cap U_j$. Hence by Sheaves,
567	Section \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} these glue to a
568	morphism of $\mathcal{O}_T$-algebras $f^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T$.
569	This proves that $F$ satisfies the sheaf condition with respect to
570	the Zariski topology.
571	
572	\medskip\noindent
573	Let $S = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ be an affine open covering.
574	Let $F_i \subset F$ be the subfunctor consisting of
575	those pairs $(f : T \to S, \varphi)$ such that
576	$f(T) \subset U_i$.
577	
578	\medskip\noindent
579	We have to show each $F_i$ is representable.
580	This is the case because $F_i$ is identified with
581	the functor associated to $U_i$ equipped with
582	the quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_{U_i}$-algebra $\mathcal{A}|_{U_i}$,
583	by Lemma \ref{lemma-spec-base-change}.
584	Thus the result follows from Lemma \ref{lemma-spec-affine}.
585	
586	\medskip\noindent
587	Next we show that $F_i \subset F$ is representable by open immersions.
588	Let $(f : T \to S, \varphi) \in F(T)$. Consider $V_i = f^{-1}(U_i)$.
589	It follows from the definition of $F_i$ that given $a : T' \to T$
590	we gave $a^*(f, \varphi) \in F_i(T')$ if and only if $a(T') \subset V_i$.
591	This is what we were required to show.
592	
593	\medskip\noindent
594	Finally, we have to show that the collection $(F_i)_{i \in I}$
595	covers $F$. Let $(f : T \to S, \varphi) \in F(T)$.
596	Consider $V_i = f^{-1}(U_i)$. Since $S = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$
597	is an open covering of $S$ we see that $T = \bigcup_{i \in I} V_i$
598	is an open covering of $T$. Moreover $(f, \varphi)|_{V_i} \in F_i(V_i)$.
599	This finishes the proof of the lemma.
600	\end{proof}
601	
602	\begin{lemma}
603	\label{lemma-glueing-gives-functor-spec}
604	In Situation \ref{situation-relative-spec}.

600	\end{proof}
601	
602	\begin{lemma}
603	\label{lemma-glueing-gives-functor-spec}
604	In Situation \ref{situation-relative-spec}.
605	The scheme $\pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \to S$
606	constructed in Lemma \ref{lemma-glue-relative-spec}
607	and the scheme representing the functor $F$ are
608	canonically isomorphic as schemes over $S$.
609	\end{lemma}
610	
611	\begin{proof}
612	Let $X \to S$ be the scheme representing the functor $F$.
613	Consider the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebras
614	$\mathcal{R} = \pi_*\mathcal{O}_{\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})}$.
615	By construction of $\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})$
616	we have isomorphisms $\mathcal{A}(U) \to \mathcal{R}(U)$
617	for every affine open $U \subset S$; this follows from
618	Lemma \ref{lemma-glue-relative-spec} part (1).
619	For $U \subset U' \subset S$ open these isomorphisms are
620	compatible with the restriction mappings; this follows from
621	Lemma \ref{lemma-glue-relative-spec} part (2).
622	Hence by Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-restrict-basis-equivalence-modules}
623	these isomorphisms result from an isomorphism of $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebras
624	$\varphi : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{R}$. Hence this gives an element
625	$(\pi, \varphi) \in F(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}))$.
626	Since $X$ represents the functor $F$ we get a corresponding
627	morphism of schemes $can : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \to X$
628	over $S$.
629	
630	\medskip\noindent
631	Let $U \subset S$ be any affine open. Let $F_U \subset F$ be
632	the subfunctor of $F$ corresponding to pairs $(f, \varphi)$ over
633	schemes $T$ with $f(T) \subset U$. Clearly the base change
634	$X_U$ represents $F_U$. Moreover, $F_U$ is represented by
635	$\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = \pi^{-1}(U)$ according to
636	Lemma \ref{lemma-spec-affine}. In other words $X_U \cong \pi^{-1}(U)$.
637	We omit the verification that this identification is brought about
638	by the base change of the morphism $can$ to $U$.
639	\end{proof}
640	
641	\begin{definition}
642	\label{definition-relative-spec}
643	Let $S$ be a scheme. Let $\mathcal{A}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf of
644	$\mathcal{O}_S$-algebras. The {\it relative spectrum of $\mathcal{A}$ over
645	$S$}, or simply the {\it spectrum of $\mathcal{A}$ over $S$} is the scheme
646	constructed in Lemma \ref{lemma-glue-relative-spec} which represents the
647	functor $F$ (\ref{equation-spec}), see
648	Lemma \ref{lemma-glueing-gives-functor-spec}.
649	We denote it $\pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \to S$.
650	The ``universal family'' is a morphism of $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebras
651	$$
652	\mathcal{A}
653	\longrightarrow
654	\pi_*\mathcal{O}_{\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})}
655	$$
656	\end{definition}
657	
658	\noindent
659	The following lemma says among other things that forming the
660	relative spectrum commutes with base change.
661	
662	\begin{lemma}
663	\label{lemma-spec-properties}
664	Let $S$ be a scheme. Let $\mathcal{A}$ be a quasi-coherent
665	sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebras. Let
666	$\pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \to S$
667	be the relative spectrum of $\mathcal{A}$ over $S$.
668	\begin{enumerate}
669	\item For every affine open $U \subset S$ the inverse image
670	$\pi^{-1}(U)$ is affine.
671	\item For every morphism $g : S' \to S$ we have
672	$S' \times_S \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) =
673	\underline{\Spec}_{S'}(g^*\mathcal{A})$.
674	\item
675	The universal map
676	$$
677	\mathcal{A}
678	\longrightarrow
679	\pi_*\mathcal{O}_{\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})}
680	$$
681	is an isomorphism of $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebras.
682	\end{enumerate}
683	\end{lemma}
684	
685	\begin{proof}
686	Part (1) comes from the description of the relative spectrum
687	by glueing, see Lemma \ref{lemma-glue-relative-spec}.
688	Part (2) follows immediately from Lemma \ref{lemma-spec-base-change}.
689	Part (3) follows because it is local on $S$ and it is clear in case $S$
690	is affine by Lemma \ref{lemma-spec-affine} for example.
691	\end{proof}
692	
693	\begin{lemma}
694	\label{lemma-canonical-morphism}
695	Let $f : X \to S$ be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated morphism
696	of schemes. By Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-push-forward-quasi-coherent}
697	the sheaf $f_*\mathcal{O}_X$ is a quasi-coherent sheaf of
698	$\mathcal{O}_S$-algebras. There is a canonical morphism
699	$$
700	can : X \longrightarrow \underline{\Spec}_S(f_*\mathcal{O}_X)
701	$$
702	of schemes over $S$.
703	For any affine open $U \subset S$ the restriction $can|_{f^{-1}(U)}$
704	is identified with the canonical morphism
705	$$
706	f^{-1}(U) \longrightarrow \Spec(\Gamma(f^{-1}(U), \mathcal{O}_X))
707	$$
708	coming from Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-morphism-into-affine}.
709	\end{lemma}
710	
711	\begin{proof}
712	The morphism comes, via the definition of $\underline{\Spec}$
713	as the scheme representing the functor $F$, from the canonical map
714	$\varphi : f^*f_*\mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_X$ (which by adjointness of
715	push and pull corresponds to
716	$\text{id} : f_*\mathcal{O}_X \to f_*\mathcal{O}_X$).
717	The statement on the restriction to $f^{-1}(U)$
718	follows from the description of the relative spectrum over
719	affines, see Lemma \ref{lemma-spec-affine}.
720	\end{proof}

732	\section{Affine n-space}
733	\label{section-affine-n-space}
734	
735	\noindent
736	As an application of the relative spectrum
737	we define affine $n$-space over a base scheme

755	\end{definition}

\chapter{Relative Spec}
\label{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}
% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean

% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-172). This chapter is the foundational A.1.a sub-row of
% Route A (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
% relative-spectrum functor \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}
% \to \mathrm{Sch}/X used by the relative Picard functor on a product C \times_k T.
% Mathlib has the absolute Spec functor and affine gluing (Scheme.GlueData,
% AffineScheme.glueOpens) but no general RelativeSpec construction; this chapter is
% the prover-ready specification for the new file AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean
% which iter-173 (file-skeleton lane) will scaffold.

\section{Setup and motivation}
\label{sec:relspec_setup}

The Route~A construction of $J = \Pic^0_{C/k}$ as a $g$-dimensional abelian variety is
organised around the relative Picard functor
$\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)$
on the category of $k$-schemes. Representing $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$ requires the line-bundle
pullback construction on a relative curve $C \times_k T \to T$, which in turn rests on a
\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras
$\mathcal{A}$, the \emph{relative spectrum}
$\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X$. This chapter packages the construction
of $\underline{\Spec}_X(-)$ and its three core properties --- universal property, affine
base, base change --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
identity-component / dimension theory of $J$ (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
(\S~``Out of scope'' below).

% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).

\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}

\begin{definition}[Quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebra]
  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Here $S$ is a scheme, and $\mathcal{A}$ is a quasi-coherent
  %  $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebra. This means that $\mathcal{A}$ is a
  %  sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
  %  $\mathcal{O}_S$-module."
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
  Let $X$ be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras} is a
  sheaf $\mathcal{A}$ of $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras (i.e. an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
  equipped with $\mathcal{O}_X$-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
  $\mathcal{O}_X$-module is quasi-coherent. We write $\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)$ for the
  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras and
  $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebra morphisms.
\end{definition}

\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
\label{sec:relspec_scheme}

The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open $U = \Spec R \subseteq X$ one
sets $\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))$ regarded as an $R$-algebra, and the local pieces
glue compatibly because $\mathcal{A}$ is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
$\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)$ for an inclusion
$V = \Spec S \subseteq U$ gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).

\begin{theorem}[Relative spectrum exists]
  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "In Situation \ref{situation-relative-spec}.
  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
  %  $$
  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
  %  $$
  %  with the following properties:
  %  \begin{enumerate}
  %  \item for every affine open $U \subset S$ there exists an isomorphism
  %  $i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))$ over $U$, and
  %  \item for $U \subset U' \subset S$ affine open the composition
  %  $$
  %  \xymatrix{
  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
  %  }
  %  $$
  %  is the open immersion of Lemma \ref{lemma-spec-inclusion} above.
  %  \end{enumerate}
  %  Moreover, $\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})$
  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over $S$."
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
  Let $X$ be a scheme and $\mathcal{A}$ a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebra
  (Definition~\ref{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}). There exists a scheme
  $\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$ together with an affine morphism
  $\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X$ such that
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item for every affine open $U \subseteq X$, there is an isomorphism
      $i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))$ over $U$;
    \item for $U \subseteq U' \subseteq X$ both affine open, the composite
      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
      $\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)$.
  \end{enumerate}
  The pair $(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)$ is unique up to unique isomorphism
  over $X$.
\end{theorem}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
% Lemmas \ref{lemma-relative-glueing},
% \ref{lemma-spec-inclusion}, and
% \ref{lemma-transitive-spec}.
% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
% Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-glueing}."
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
  affine local pieces $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))$ for each affine open $U \subseteq X$ and
  open-immersion transition data
  $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$ for $U \subseteq U'$
  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
  $\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)$; quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
  $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$, so each transition is an
  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
\end{proof}

\begin{theorem}[Universal property of the relative spectrum]
  \label{thm:relative_spec_univ}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.UniversalProperty}
  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras, thm:relative_spec_exists}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ continuation: Section ``Relative spectrum as
  % a functor'', functor F and Lemma lemma-spec
  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L427-L466 + L547-L551).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "For any $f : T \to S$ the pullback
  %  $f^*\mathcal{A}$ is a quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_T$-algebras.
  %  We are going to consider pairs $(f : T \to S, \varphi)$ where
  %  $f$ is a morphism of schemes and $\varphi : f^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T$
  %  is a morphism of $\mathcal{O}_T$-algebras. Note that this is the
  %  same as giving a $f^{-1}\mathcal{O}_S$-algebra homomorphism
  %  $\varphi : f^{-1}\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T$, see
  %  Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-adjointness-tensor-restrict}.
  %  This is also the same as giving an $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebra map
  %  $\varphi : \mathcal{A} \to f_*\mathcal{O}_T$, see
  %  Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-adjoint-push-pull-modules}.
  %  We will use all three ways of thinking about $\varphi$,
  %  without further mention. [...]
  %  In this way we have defined a functor
  %  \begin{eqnarray}
  %  \label{equation-spec}
  %  F : \Sch^{opp} & \longrightarrow & \textit{Sets} \\
  %  T & \longmapsto & F(T) = \{\text{pairs }(f, \varphi) \text{ as above}\}
  %  \nonumber
  %  \end{eqnarray}
  %  [...]
  %  The functor $F$ is representable by a scheme."
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-spec + section-spec
  functor description); [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(b).}
  Let $X$ be a scheme and $\mathcal{A}$ a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebra.
  Then $\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$ represents the functor
  $F : \Sch^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}$,
  $T \mapsto F(T) := \{(f, \varphi) : f : T \to X \text{ a morphism},\;
  \varphi : f^* \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T \text{ an } \mathcal{O}_T\text{-algebra map}\}.$
  Equivalently, for any $X$-scheme $g : T \to X$,
  there is a natural bijection
  \[
    \Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \;\;\cong\;\;
    \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_* \mathcal{O}_T)
  \]
  (using the adjunction between $g_*$ and $g^*$ to convert between
  $\mathcal{A} \to g_*\mathcal{O}_T$ and $g^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T$).
  The bijection is natural in $T$.
\end{theorem}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: TODO retrieve from references/stacks-constructions.tex
% (proof of lemma-spec, L553-L600, ~50 lines covering the Zariski sheaf property,
% the subfunctors F_i associated to an affine cover, and identifying each F_i
% with the affine-base case via lemma-spec-base-change and lemma-spec-affine).
% Verbatim quote omitted for length; the structural steps are restated below in
% project notation.
\begin{proof}
  \uses{thm:relative_spec_exists, thm:relative_spec_affine_base, thm:relative_spec_base_change}
  Following Stacks tag 01LQ (proof of lemma-spec): the functor $F$ is a Zariski sheaf
  because morphisms of schemes and $\mathcal{O}$-algebra maps both glue under open covers.
  Choose an affine open cover $X = \bigcup U_i$. The subfunctor
  $F_i \subseteq F$ of pairs $(f, \varphi)$ with $f(T) \subseteq U_i$ is representable by
  $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U_i))$ via the affine-base case
  (Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}) together with the
  base-change identification $F_i \cong F_{U_i,\,\mathcal{A}|_{U_i}}$
  (Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_base_change}). Each $F_i \hookrightarrow F$ is an open
  subfunctor (pull back $U_i$ along $f$). The $F_i$ jointly cover $F$ since the $U_i$
  cover $X$. The gluing of representable open subfunctors produces a representing scheme,
  which is canonically isomorphic to the glued scheme $\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$
  of Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_exists}.
\end{proof}

\section{Affine base}
\label{sec:relspec_affine}

\begin{theorem}[Relative spectrum over an affine base]
  \label{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.affine_base_iff}
  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras, thm:relative_spec_exists}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LO (lemma-spec-affine)
  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L491-L545).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "In Situation \ref{situation-relative-spec}.
  %  Let $F$ be the functor associated to $(S, \mathcal{A})$ above.
  %  If $S$ is affine, then $F$ is representable by the
  %  affine scheme $\Spec(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{A}))$."
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LO (lemma-spec-affine).}
  Let $X = \Spec R$ be an affine scheme and let $\mathcal{A}$ be a quasi-coherent
  $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebra. Set $A := \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})$, regarded as an
  $R$-algebra. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of $X$-schemes
  \[
    \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \;\;\cong\;\; \Spec(A).
  \]
  In particular, on an affine base the relative spectrum reduces to the absolute
  spectrum of the global sections.
\end{theorem}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Write $S = \Spec(R)$ and $A = \Gamma(S, \mathcal{A})$.
% Then $A$ is an $R$-algebra and $\mathcal{A} = \widetilde A$.
% The ring map $R \to A$ gives rise to a canonical map
% $$
% f_{univ} : \Spec(A)
% \longrightarrow
% S = \Spec(R).
% $$
% We have
% $f_{univ}^*\mathcal{A} =  \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}$
% by Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback}.
% Hence there is a canonical map
% $$
% \varphi_{univ} :
% f_{univ}^*\mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}
% \longrightarrow
% \widetilde A = \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(A)}
% $$
% coming from the $A$-module map $A \otimes_R A \to A$,
% $a \otimes a' \mapsto aa'$. We claim that the pair
% $(f_{univ}, \varphi_{univ})$ represents $F$ in this case."
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras, thm:relative_spec_univ}
  Write $X = \Spec R$ and $A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})$. Quasi-coherence of
  $\mathcal{A}$ gives $\mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A}$ as $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
  (with the $R$-algebra structure on $A$ inducing the algebra structure on
  $\widetilde A$). The ring map $R \to A$ gives a morphism
  $f_{\mathrm{univ}} : \Spec A \to \Spec R = X$ and a canonical
  $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A}$-algebra map
  $\varphi_{\mathrm{univ}} : f_{\mathrm{univ}}^* \mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}
  \to \mathcal{O}_{\Spec A} = \widetilde A$ given by multiplication
  $a \otimes a' \mapsto aa'$. We verify $(\Spec A, f_{\mathrm{univ}}, \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}})$
  represents $F$ on the affine base: given a test scheme $T$ and a pair
  $(f, \varphi)$, the composite
  $A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{f^\sharp}
  \Gamma(T, f^*\mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{\varphi} \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)$
  is a ring map $A \to \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)$ and hence corresponds to a morphism
  $T \to \Spec A$ by the affine universal property
  (Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.toSpec\_naturality} /
  \texttt{Scheme.Hom.toSpec}). One checks this assignment inverts the universal map
  $(f, \varphi) \mapsto a$ defined by $f = f_{\mathrm{univ}} \circ a$,
  $\varphi = a^* \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}}$.
\end{proof}

\section{Base change}
\label{sec:relspec_basechange}

\begin{theorem}[Base change of the relative spectrum]
  \label{thm:relative_spec_base_change}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.base_change}
  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras, thm:relative_spec_exists}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LS (lemma-spec-base-change and
  % lemma-spec-properties part (2))
  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L467-L489 + L662-L691).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "In Situation \ref{situation-relative-spec}.
  %  Let $F$ be the functor
  %  associated to $(S, \mathcal{A})$ above.
  %  Let $g : S' \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
  %  Set $\mathcal{A}' = g^*\mathcal{A}$. Let $F'$ be the
  %  functor associated to $(S', \mathcal{A}')$ above.
  %  Then there is a canonical isomorphism
  %  $$
  %  F' \cong h_{S'} \times_{h_S} F
  %  $$
  %  of functors. [...]
  %  For every morphism $g : S' \to S$ we have
  %  $S' \times_S \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) =
  %  \underline{\Spec}_{S'}(g^*\mathcal{A})$."
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LS (lemma-spec-base-change +
  lemma-spec-properties).}
  Let $g : T \to X$ be a morphism of schemes and let $\mathcal{A}$ be a quasi-coherent
  $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebra. Then $g^* \mathcal{A}$ is a quasi-coherent
  $\mathcal{O}_T$-algebra and there is a canonical isomorphism of $T$-schemes
  \[
    T \times_X \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \;\;\cong\;\;
    \underline{\Spec}_T(g^* \mathcal{A}).
  \]
\end{theorem}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "A pair $(f' : T \to S', \varphi' : (f')^*\mathcal{A}' \to \mathcal{O}_T)$
% is the same as a pair $(f, \varphi : f^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T)$
% together with a factorization of $f$ as $f = g \circ f'$. Namely with
% this notation we have
% $(f')^* \mathcal{A}' = (f')^*g^*\mathcal{A} = f^*\mathcal{A}$.
% Hence the lemma."
\begin{proof}
  \uses{thm:relative_spec_univ}
  By Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_univ}, $\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$ represents
  the functor $F$ sending an $X$-scheme $h : S \to X$ to the set of
  $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebra maps $h^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_S$. A morphism
  $S \to T \times_X \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$ over $T$ is the same as a pair of an
  $X$-morphism $S \to \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$ together with a $T$-factorisation
  of $S \to X$, i.e.\ a morphism $S \to T$ whose composition with $g$ equals
  $S \to X$. Tracing through, this is a $T$-morphism $f' : S \to T$ together with an
  $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebra map $(f')^*(g^*\mathcal{A}) = f^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_S$.
  This is precisely $F'(S)$, the functor for $(T, g^*\mathcal{A})$, so the canonical map
  $T \times_X \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to \underline{\Spec}_T(g^*\mathcal{A})$
  is an isomorphism by Yoneda.
\end{proof}

\section{Functoriality}
\label{sec:relspec_functoriality}

\begin{theorem}[Relative spectrum as a functor]
  \label{thm:relative_spec_functorial}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.functor}
  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras, thm:relative_spec_exists, thm:relative_spec_univ}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LR (definition-relative-spec +
  % lemma-glueing-gives-functor-spec + lemma-spec-properties part (3))
  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L602-L691).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Let $S$ be a scheme. Let $\mathcal{A}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf of
  %  $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebras. The {\it relative spectrum of $\mathcal{A}$ over
  %  $S$}, or simply the {\it spectrum of $\mathcal{A}$ over $S$} is the scheme
  %  constructed in Lemma \ref{lemma-glue-relative-spec} which represents the
  %  functor $F$ (\ref{equation-spec}), see
  %  Lemma \ref{lemma-glueing-gives-functor-spec}.
  %  We denote it $\pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \to S$.
  %  The ``universal family'' is a morphism of $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebras
  %  $$
  %  \mathcal{A}
  %  \longrightarrow
  %  \pi_*\mathcal{O}_{\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})}
  %  $$
  %  [...]
  %  The universal map
  %  $$
  %  \mathcal{A}
  %  \longrightarrow
  %  \pi_*\mathcal{O}_{\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})}
  %  $$
  %  is an isomorphism of $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebras."
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LR (definition-relative-spec +
  lemma-glueing-gives-functor-spec + lemma-spec-properties);
  [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(b).}
  The construction $\mathcal{A} \mapsto \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$ assembles into
  a contravariant functor
  \[
    \underline{\Spec}_X : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathrm{AffSch}/X
  \]
  from quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras to affine $X$-schemes: a morphism of
  $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras $\psi : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ induces, by the universal
  property (Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_univ}) applied to the pair
  $(\pi_\mathcal{B}, \psi \circ \pi_\mathcal{B}^*) :
  \pi_\mathcal{B}^*\mathcal{A} \to \pi_\mathcal{B}^*\mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{O}_{\Spec\mathcal{B}}$,
  an $X$-morphism
  $\underline{\Spec}_X(\psi) : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{B}) \to \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$.
  Functoriality (identity, composition) is direct from the universal property. The
  pushforward $\pi_* \mathcal{O}_{\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})} \cong \mathcal{A}$
  exhibits $\underline{\Spec}_X$ as an equivalence between $\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}$ and
  the full subcategory of affine $X$-schemes, with inverse $f \mapsto f_* \mathcal{O}_Y$
  (Stacks lemma-spec-properties part~(3)).
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{thm:relative_spec_exists, thm:relative_spec_univ, thm:relative_spec_affine_base}
  Functoriality follows directly from Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_univ}: for
  $\psi : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$, the pair
  $(\pi_\mathcal{B} : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{B}) \to X, \;
  \pi_\mathcal{B}^*(\psi))$ defines an $X$-morphism
  $\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{B}) \to \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$ via the
  representing universal pair. Identity and composition follow from naturality of the
  representing bijection in $\mathcal{A}$. For the equivalence claim, $\underline{\Spec}_X$
  is fully faithful because the isomorphism
  $\pi_* \mathcal{O}_{\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})} \cong \mathcal{A}$
  (locally identifying with $\Gamma(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)),\mathcal{O}) = \mathcal{A}(U)$
  via Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}) reconstructs $\mathcal{A}$ from
  $\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$. Essential surjectivity onto affine $X$-schemes uses
  the canonical morphism $X \to \underline{\Spec}_X(\pi_*\mathcal{O}_X)$ of Stacks
  lemma-canonical-morphism; on an affine $X$-scheme $\pi : Y \to X$, this canonical
  morphism is an isomorphism (affine-locally it is the identity
  $\Spec\Gamma(\pi^{-1}(U), \mathcal{O}_Y) \to \Spec\Gamma(\pi^{-1}(U), \mathcal{O}_Y)$).
\end{proof}

\section{Lean encoding}
\label{sec:relspec_lean_encoding}

The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}.
The construction proceeds by gluing absolute affine pieces: for each affine open
$U \subseteq X$ with $U = \Spec R$ and $A := \mathcal{A}(U)$ regarded as an
$R$-algebra, the local piece is $\Spec A$ with structure morphism $\Spec A \to \Spec R = U$
induced by the algebra unit $R \to A$. Mathlib provides \texttt{IsAffineOpen.toScheme} for
the affine-open inclusion and the absolute \texttt{Scheme.Spec} functor for the local
pieces; the transition isomorphisms come from quasi-coherence of $\mathcal{A}$ via
\texttt{QuasiCoherent.tensorIso} (or the explicit
$\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)$ for $V = \Spec S \subseteq U$).
The gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData} (general construction) or
the convenience wrapper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme.glueOpens}; the cocycle
condition reduces to Stacks \texttt{lemma-transitive-spec}. The universal property
(Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_univ}) is expressed as a
\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} witness, providing the natural bijection
$\Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \cong
\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_*\mathcal{O}_T)$ as a
\texttt{Yoneda}-style structure. The base-change isomorphism
(Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_base_change}) and functoriality
(Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_functorial}) are derived from the universal property by
Yoneda. No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a definitional/structural
construction on top of existing Mathlib affine-scheme infrastructure.

\section{Out of scope}
\label{sec:relspec_out_of_scope}

This chapter packages \emph{only} the relative-spectrum construction and its three core
properties. The following downstream constructions live in separate sibling chapters and
are explicitly out of scope here:
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Relative Picard functor $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$} (A.1.c) --- consumed-by, not
    defined-here; its blueprint home is the planned chapter
    \texttt{Picard\_RelPicFunctor.tex}.
  \item \textbf{Line-bundle pullback on a product $C \times_k T$} (A.1.b) ---
    \texttt{Picard\_LineBundlePullback.tex} (planned).
  \item \textbf{Connectedness, dimensionality, smoothness of $\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$}
    --- downstream properties that depend on assumptions on $\mathcal{A}$
    (locally finite type, flat, etc.), not unconditional consequences of the construction.
  \item \textbf{Identity-component $\Pic^0$ and abelian-variety structure} (A.3) --- a
    separate Route~A sub-row, fed by but not constructed in this chapter.
\end{itemize}
